items.q: Does the padlock (HTTPS) icon mean a website is trustworthy?
items.correct: No - it only means the connection is encrypted; criminals can obtain certificates too, so a phishing site can still show the padlock.
items.misconception: Yes - the padlock proves the site's owners are honest.
items.random_wrong: Yes - phishing sites cannot use HTTPS.
items.why: Encryption gets confused with endorsement. The padlock secures the channel to the site - it says nothing about who is at the other end.

items.q: Does a strong password protect you from phishing?
items.correct: Not by itself - phishing tricks you into handing the password over, so its strength is irrelevant; spotting fraudulent messages and using two-factor authentication help.
items.misconception: Yes - a long complex password cannot be phished.
items.random_wrong: Yes - phishing only works on passwords shorter than 8 characters.
items.why: Password strength counters brute force, a different attack. Matching each defence to the threat it addresses is the core skill in security questions.

items.q: Are phones and Macs immune to malware?
items.correct: No - any device running software can run malicious software; attackers target whatever platforms are popular, including mobile devices.
items.misconception: Yes - malware is only written for Windows PCs.
items.random_wrong: Yes - app stores make malware technically impossible.
items.why: Historic market share created the myth. Reduced exposure is not immunity - mobile banking trojans and Mac malware are well documented.

items.q: An email pretending to be from your bank, asking you to confirm your password, is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Phishing. “Defragmentation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Systematically trying many possible passwords until one succeeds
items.why: It's Systematically trying many possible passwords until one succeeds. “Physically breaking the computer case” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The role of a firewall is to:
items.correct: Monitor and filter network traffic according to security rules
items.why: Correct answer: Monitor and filter network traffic according to security rules. “Speed up the processor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which defence makes intercepted data unreadable to an attacker?
items.why: Correct answer: Encryption. “A bigger monitor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A PIN is made of 4 digits, each 0-9. How many different PINs are possible?
items.why: Exact answer: 10000 combinations. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
